Vor. VI. MARCH, 1915. No. 8

INSTRUCTIONS OFF KING CORMAC—THE IRISH SOLOMON.-

E From a Paper read hefore the Irish Laterary Society.

Qarbery: ‘‘Cormac, Grandson of Conn,; what dues hath a Chief in an
) Ale-house ? *’

; Cormac: ‘‘Not hard to tell!

i Good behaviour around a good chief;

Lamps to light for the eyes’ relief;
BExerting oneself for the Company’s sake,

Seats assigned with no clownish mistake ; |

Deft and liberal-measuring carvers; =713
Attentive and nimble-handed servers;

- Moderation in music and song,

A telling of Stories mot too long;

The Host, to a bright elation stirred, :

Giving each guest a welcoming word. B, 1

Silence during the Bard’s reciting, S f

Pach chorus in sweet concert umiting.”’ S n

garbeﬂ'y: ‘“ 0 Cormac, Grandson of Conm, say sooth; S5 \

How didst thou order thy ways in youth?

Cormac: ‘‘Into the woods I went a-listeming; S
' I was a gazer when stars were glistening; s i
Blind when secrets were plain to guess;

A Silent Oune in the Wilderness; s
I was talkative with the many,
Yet, in the Mead-hall, milder than any; &
I was stern amid battle cmies; : g
x 1 was gentle towards allies; | j
B ' I was a doctor unto the sick; 4 -:-5{.
f' On the feeble I laid no stick. S &

A - . »

T R W ek TN W e D e ey
‘ ’ : B

THE IRISH BOOK LOVER.

Not close, lest burdensome I should be;

Though wise, not prone to arrogancy.

I promised little though lavish of gift;

I never was reckless though I was swift;
Young, I never derided the old;

And never boasted though I was bold;

Of an absent one no evil would tell;

Nor ever reproached, though I praised full well;
I never would ask but ever would give;

For a Kingly Life I craved to live.”

‘““ Cormac, Comnn’s grandson, and Son of Great Art,
Declare to me now from the depths of thy heart,
With the wise and the foolish,
With strangers and friends,
The meek and the mulish,
The old and the young,
With good manners to make God amends,
How I must govern my tongue,
And in all things comport myself purely
The good and the wicked among.’”’

““ The answer thereto is not difficult, surely.

Be not too wise nor too scatter-brained,

Not too oconceited nor too mestrained,

Be not too haughty nor yet too meek,

Too tattle-tongued or too loth to speak,

Neither too hard nor yet too weak.

If too wise ym:n appear, folk too much will claim of you,
If too foolish they still will be making fresh game of you,
If too oonceited vexatious they’ll dub you,

If too unselfish they only will snub you,

If too much of a tattler you ne'er will be heeded,

If too silent your company ne’er will be needed,

If over hard, your pride will be broken “amnd-elr '

If over weak, the foll: will trample you under.”

ALFRED PERCEVAL G—RAYES

MR. E. McO. DIX AND THE HISTORY OF IRISH PRINTING.

The study of the history of local printing has much iﬁterest, as
apart from its help towards a general wegister of publications, light
is very often thrown on the history of newspapers and periodicals, and,
perhaps mone important than anything else, it enables many hitherto
undated pamphlets and books to be assigned a date which 1s often of
real historical value. There has been little done in- England and Wales
on this subject; some work in Scotland, but attemtion must now be
drawn to the magnificent work that one man has done for Ireland.
Mr. Ermest McClintock Dix, a Dublin solicitor, stamted on this work
of registering all the books, pamphlets and newspapers printed in |
Ireland about 1898. He hegan collecting these items, and now he &
has a most wonderful library of early Dublin, and later, provincisl
imprints. From these collections and from those in the Royal Irish
Academy and Trinity College he has compiled over seventy articles &
and pamphlets on the history of printing in the various Irish towns. : ;'
He first dealt with Dublin up to the year 1700, including, of course,
the very interesting and early hooks printed in the 16th century in
the Irish character. Then a series of Irish bibliographical pamphlets
was issued dealing with the other large towns, amd giving complet®
lists of their publications to the year 1800. A long series of articles
dealt. with all the smaller towns and villages in which printing was.
egbablished bofore 1800, and them Mr. Dix, oraving for fresh fields,
began work on the period 1800-1825. Ho has also published two
editions of a gazetteer of Irish printing, several momographs on early
Dublin printers end their initials, marks, etc., and with Mr. Casaide

has issued a list of books printed in the Irish language from the 16th 5

THE. IRISH BOOK LOVIER: 125

century to 1820, wherever printed. His check list of Dublin news-
papers onables the student to find in which of the Dublin libraries any
purtxioula:r volume of any paper exists.

A large number of Mr. Dix’s articles have been published in ‘‘ The
Irish Book Lover,”’ an excellent little journal published in London by
that enthusiastic doctor, magistrate, library committeeman, politician,
pibliographer, and above all Irishmaam, Dr. J. 8. Crone. Owing to the
kindness of Mr. Dix and Dr. Crone, there is in the St. Bride Typo-
graph ical Library as complete a set as it 1s possible to make of the
articles and pamphlets by Mrv. Dix, and in the printed catalogue of
that Library, which is now in the press, the list of his writings occupies
a large space.

The work that Mr. Dix has done for Ireland shows what one man
e do when he is really interested, even in his spare time. When
our young librarians discover that the pursuit of local imprints 18
really very amusing, and that the local press is always prepared to
print lists of such books, and in some cases Library. Committees may
be induced to print such articles 1n their Annual Report (as Tyne-
mouth and North Shields did some years ago), they may wake up and
do for their. district the work that M. Dix has wundertaken and
accomplished for the whole of Ireland .—The Library World.

« PTHE CORK MAGAZINE.”

This periodical was founded in November, 1847, and ran for twelve

months, its final number being that for Ootober, 1848. It was publish-
ed by Bradford & Co., of Patrick Street, and it bears on its first title

page the names of McGlashan, of Dublin; Simms and Meclntyre, of

- Belfagt, and J. Gilbert, of London. With the secomd number Vernon,

are replaced by Lamont Brothers,

T think I may safely conjecture, was J. L. Forrest, the Cork poet. -

to Trinity.” |
P Part 10 mtrodruoee a writer who efterwards became very motable in  §=
America, namely, Fitzjames O’Brien, whose collected work in prose and
yerse, edited by William ‘Winter, shows remarkable power and skill,
end indicates whet a serious loss his early death inflicted on Irish
literature. Another long poem by *J. de Jeam,” amnd a shorter oue
. ISEE by Mulchinock, are among the other pieces. ‘‘J.R.” (clearly James
i Roche) criticises O’Neill Daunt’s ‘* Persomal Recollections of 0'Cor-

there is no indication in the numben itself, No.-12 appears to have

IRISH LITERARY SOCIETY. |
The Annual Reception of the Society was held on Saturday, 16th

J ﬁnulary. There was a crowded and distinguished: assemblage of

visitors and friends of many mnationalities, and Miss Eleanor Hull,
Vice-President, graciously acted as hostess. The entertainment atter-
wards took the form of an Irish-Belgian concert, and an interesting
prdgtramme was submitted, the various items of which were enthusias-
tically applauded. Mr. Samuel Boyle welcomed the guests in a neat
speeck in Gaelic and English. Mr. Plunkett Greene, Miss Jean Ster-
ling MacKinlay, Mr. Owen Colyer, and Miss Mary O’Sullivan con-

tributed some quaint old Irish ditties with all their usual verve, whilst

Mr. Guy Pentwee wrecited ome of Mangan’s stirring ballads. An

- especial feature was the rendering by Mr. Ernest Bertram and Mass

Marie Brema. of some Belgian ballads and Flemish folk songs, both in
the original tongue and in excellent English translations, prepared for
the occasion by Mr. Alfred Perceval Graves. A collection on behalf
of the Belgian refugees realised £25, which was sent to M. Verheran,
the famous Belgian poet.
At a Tea-Table Talk on 8rd February Mr. Alfred P. Graves delighted
a large and appreciative audience with a charming paper on ‘‘An
Irish Solomon.”” Some poetical extracts from which we are enabled, by
the kindness of Mr. Giraves, to present to our readers on another page.
~ It was announced that for an indefinite time the Society will lose
the active and energetic services of its indefatigable and ubiquitous
honorary secretary, Mr. C. R. Cooke-Taylor, Barrister-at-Law, who has
gone -to-Serbia in charge of Lady Paget’s Hospital Ambulance. M.
Lynam, the equally efficient honorary librarian, is on active service

- pomewhere in France, but a promised paper by him on *‘ Emily Law-

less ”’ has been received, and will be submitted at an early Tea-Table.

NATIONAL LITERARY SOCIETY.

At a meeting of the Society on 8th February, over which Mr.
Fdward Martyn presided, and which was well attended, Mr. R. J.
Kelly, K.C., read a paper on ‘‘Thomas Dermody, a Clare Poet.” He
said that Dermody was almost unknown in their latter day literature.
A few pieces by him, sometimes not chosen with the best judgment,
were to be found in some rare collections, but they gave one no idea
of his genius. He had the real gift of poetry, and the divine flame
burned in his soul. In mamy things he had mmuch in common with
Burns. Had he a little more resolution, was he not so easily swayed
by every passing fancy, so weak and wayward, he would undoubtedly
have left works comparable to the best English classics. As a child
he was an infant prodigy and an intellectual pheomenon. He could
read and translate books in Greek and Latin when only eight years
of age, and some of his best pieces were composed before he had
reached the age of twelve. When he left Ennis, where he had been
assisting his father as a teacher in a classical school, he csme ‘o Dub-
lin, where he became the spoiled darling of the drawingrooms. In
London he took to jourmalism, but found he could write poetry better
and quicker than prose. He wrote some political pamphlets, one in
1793, being entitled ‘“ On the Rights and Justice of National Liberty.”’
In January, 1800, his friends induced him to publish his poems. He
did so, and the book was dedicated to his truest friend, Lady Moira.
Dermody died in 1802 at the age of twenty-seven and a half years, and
wag buried at Lewisham. The inscription on his tombstone was from
his fine poem, ‘‘ The Fate of Genius.”

EDITOR’S GOSSIP.

Baroness Dorchester, Greywell, Winch field, Hampshire, a descend-
ant of that famous Tyrone soldier, Sir Guy Carleton, would feel much
obliged if anyome could send her the words of the old Irish hunting

song “The Red Fox’’ (Modhereen-a-rua). .There have been many

versions of this song. That Owenson shone in it, over a century ago,
we know from Maginn’s address to Ledy Morgan in * Fraser’s Maga-
zine?”’ for January, 1886; ‘‘ all for him who to the shady grove has
gone for years a score,—for the sake of old Macowen, and his song of

' Modereen Roo’—for your father’s sake we are going never more. o

bother you.” The best I ever heard was one SUNg, many a year agy
by an intinerant musician outside the hotel at Mucruss, who rattleq
off the praises of ‘‘ Bluebell, Sxveefhps, Towler,”” at am astonishing
rate. |

It 1s ocurious how the old air has beoome conmected with two great
national leaders. Every reader knows how Emmet started up at Moore's
playing, and wished he were leading a charge of twenty thousand men
to its inspiring strains, and on the last visit Parmell paid to Co. Sligo
he drove to Inniscrone headed by a piper playing the same old tune.

Sir Wm. Robertson Nicoll tells a little literary love story in a
recent issue of ‘‘The British Weekly.”” He published in the ‘‘ Book-
man ’’ some time ago a lyric sent in for ‘one of the oom,ﬁpetitions. This
attracted the admiration of a young Irish lady, who sent am appreci-
ative letter to the author. Other correspondence followed, and the
result is that the two are now engaged to be married.

‘ Great Thoughts’ for 30th January contains a portrait and an

appreciation of W. B. Yeats from the pen of the editor, Rev. Dr.

Downes, well done on the whole, but marred by the thrice repeated

solecism ‘‘ the Countess of Cathleen.”” He says, ‘‘ The unsubstantial
syren songs peculiar to his Celtic temperament remder Mr. Yeats for
the most part the poet of the few. Romantic Ireland finds consider-
able delight in him, but the Englishman is bewildered by much which
he has produced and the lowland Scot utterly impervious to its elusive

charm. The fact is thait his verse is characteristically Irish both in its

choice of themes and his manner of treatment.’”’

Our Irish poets are much in evidence in the February magazines,
Mr. Perceval Graves gives a fine musical rendering of ‘‘The Sea God's
Address to Bran,”’ from * The Voyage of Bran,’”’ in the ‘Fortnightly.”
Katherine Tynan sings * The Temple’’ and Thomas Walsh the ‘ Holy
Wells ”” in the ‘‘ British,”” and 8. R. Lysaght contributes a sonnet to

the ‘‘ English”’ in which also Robert Lynd describes * Ireland in War

Crosses ”’ to ‘‘ The Antiquary,”’ and Lord Dunsany ‘' A Story of Lﬂ-ﬂd
‘and Sea’”’ to ‘‘The Forum.”

- The death is announced of the Rev. Ralph Wm. Harden, formerly
Rector of St. John’s, Monkstown, Co. Dublin. Despite total blindness
he wrote and published a history of his parish which evidenced much
aocurate research and considerable literary ability.

FroM FArR Lanps. Poems of North and South. By ‘‘Gervais
Gage”’ (J. Lawrence Remtoul). (Macmillan.) &s. This handsome
volume of verse by an antipodean member of the great intellectual
Ulster family of the Rentouls, now a professor in Melbourne University,
comes to us heralded by warm words of appreciation from no less a
critic than the late Prof. Dowden. It includes poems dealing with
and describing scenes and incidents in Ireland—still the home of his
 beart—and in the land of his adoption, of which he writes with pride ;
of the men who have influenced him; with the long thoughts of youth
- and the reveries of age. One of tahe best is a striking poetic recollec-
“tion and appreciation written after visiting the grave of Sam Perry,
| Who greatly influenced him for good, when life was most impression-
"-~ able, Of him he says:—

i ‘“ You paid your price; and you wemnt your way,
f | Amnd you feared no man ; and you cursed no foe;
'"Twas prize enough at the close of day,
Your wife’s glad face and her eyes aglow.”
"‘- joyous, uplifting, spirit-stirring book, a better companion for any

a riverside lodging house, spoiled by her father and w orshipped py
“ Lennie next door’’ (a typical product of our elementary schoo}
system), suddenly finds herself thrust into aristocratic society g
secretary to a lady with literany ambitions. During a stay west of the
Shannon, where the landscape and the smell of the turf arouses in hep
strange feelings of a former ecxistence, she is loved by, and loves iy
return, Hugh Delmege, a fine type of the landlord “class, but how the
course of their love runs it would not be fair to disclose. The author
~ bas evolved in her a singularly winsome and original creature; whilst
her descriptions of low and high class life in London and Connaught
ring true and delight the reader. Her clear, concise delineation of
oharacter, and an excellent prose style, lift the work high above the
~ average novel, and leads one to look for even better work in the future.
Lorp CraANpDOoNNELL. By S. M. Christina. (Washbourne.) 2.
The scemne of this pleasantly written little tale opens in an old castle
in dark Donegal, shifting to Italy, America, Rosstrevor, and back
again. The incidents of the secret marriage, the lost papers, the
- modest maiden singing before royalty, and the rightful heir restored to
_his own almost by a miracle, smack somewhat of the ‘‘Family Herald"
and betray the prentice hand. But it is told with such a touching
faith and simplicity, such a pious outlook on life, such an atmosphere
~of Innocence, that criticism stands disarmed before it as o mailed
- warrior becfb-re a toddling babe.

%* * Mary Frances Ousack, or the “Nun of Kenmare,” wag ,
voluminous author and transtator. The following is a fairly complots
list of her published works: 1868, An illustrated history of Irelanq
1870, The Student’s Manual of Irish History. 1871, A History of the
Kingdom of Kerry. 1872, The Life of St. Aloysius Gonzaga; The
 Liberator, His Life and Times, 2 vols., (another edition, 1877). 1873
Books for Spiritual Reading, Kenmare Series. 1875, A History of the
Oity and County of Cork; Devotions for public and private use. 1876,
In Memoriam, Mary O’Hagan. 1877, Tim O’Halloran’s Choice, or from
Killarney to New York; Good reading for Girls; A Nun's Advice to
her Girls; The Trias Thaumaturga; A History of the Irish Nation;
The Life of Saint Patrick, Apostle of Ireland. 1878, Lafe of Most Rev
Joseph Dixon, D.D., Primate of all Ireland. 1880, The Apparition af
Knook. 1881, The Present Case of Ireland stated; Cloister Songs.
1882, Three Visits to Knock. 1883, Devotions for Pilgrims to Knock;;
A Souvenir of Knock; 'Pra,yers and other devotioms. 1887, The Ques-
tion of To-day, Amnti-Poverty and Progress. 1888, The Nun of Ken-
mare, an autobiography; (another edition, 1889). 1889, ILafe inside
the Church of Rome. 1891, The Story of My Life. 1892, A Remark-
able Book and two remarkable relics; What Rome Teaches. 18967
The Black Pope, a History of the Jesuits. (1897), His Yarn, and
another, story ; Is there a Roman Catholic Church? Life and Times of
Pope Pius IX.; Ned Rusheen; The Spouse of Christ, 3 vols.; Woman's
Work in M,odeu'n Society ; Jesus and Jerusalem ; Life of Father Mathew.

O. J. Kickham (*‘Phil Ormond’’) addressed to Darby Ryan. M,
McCall is of opinion that there were two poets of the same name,
Darby Ryan, senior and junior. The tradition in Sean O’Gruagaing
~ family is that Darby Ryan the poet was the son of James Ryan, whe
. married a Miss Hally, and that the poet’s wife was Mary Desmond,

Cork. SEAMUS O’CASAIDE.

ArTEUR O’SHAUGHNESSY. Could you inform me where I could obtaip
a poem written by the blind poet Pﬂlihhp Bourke Marston on the annj.
versary of the death of his brother-in-law, Arthur O’Shaughnessy?

Belfast. EUSTACE GORDON.

* ¥ It 1s contained in his last volume ‘‘ Wind Voices,”” published
by Blliott Stock. O. 1885, ‘

PrintTiNG IN ATHLONE (Vol. VI., p. 106). May I add that Danie}
Daly printed an edition of the ‘‘ Catholic Directory for the Diocese of
Elphin ”’ for the year 1804. See Remehan’s ‘¢ Collections on Irish
Church History,” Vol. I., p. 319. |

Enniscorthy. W. H. G, F.

| FORTHCOMING WORKS.

Samuel Burdy’s ‘‘ Life of Philip Skelton,’”” which Macaulay regarded
as the best picture of Irish life published in the eighteenth century,
has been reprinted from the oniginal edition of 1792, and will shortly
be issued by the Oxford University Press under the edltorslup of Dr.
Norman Moore.

I have already drawn. attenfion to the work of & new humorist,
Hon. Alexis Roche, brother of Lord Fermoy, whose ‘‘Journeys with
Jerry the Jarvey’ have heen appearing in the ‘‘Cornhill Magazine.”
Messrs. Smith, Elder have in preparation a complete volume of these
sketches. I understand that Mr. Roche was discovered by Canom
Hannay, and urged by him te give literary form to the good stories
he told. |

Mr. Robert Lynd has a new work nearly ceady bearing the some-
what curious title of ‘‘The Book of This and That.”” Messrs. Mills
and Boon will publish it at 4s. 6d. net. |

The Catholic Truth Society will shortly issue Dr. Grattan Flood’s
new musical edition of ‘‘ The Armagh Hymmnal.”

Messrs. Hodder and Stoughton amnounce a neiv work of - fiotion
from the pen of ‘‘CGleongo Birmingham,” entitled ‘‘ Bertha’s Bishop:
and Other Stories.”
